content.full_text:
Company's Wage and Salary to Encourage Political Activity Requires
Written Records

The Eller Advertising Company plans to encourage its
employees to participate in certain designated campaigns up
to 10% of their compensated time. Under §162 §, §ller will
have made a contribution or expenditure if an employee spends
more than 10% of his compensated time working on political
activities. The staff advised Eller that its employees should
keep some type of written record to insure that those employees
who do spend more than 10% of their compensated time working
on political activities will be reported by Eller.

78-315

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Participants                Date : June 8, 1978

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Barbara Campbell

Subject: Eller Outdoor Advertising's Employees' Services as Contributions

As you may recall, several weeks ago we considered a request from Chip Nielsen concerning the reporting of employee salary payments as an in-kind contribution from Eller Outdoor Advertising ("Eller").

As a follow up to my response, Chip Nielsen called with the following additional information. Eller is going to get involved in local elections where there is an ordinance or measure banning bill boards. Eller will be identifying these specific elections for which it wants its employees to campaign in opposition to the measure. Therefore an employee will be rendering services for a political purpose "at the request or direction of the employer" and the salary payments will be reportable if an employee spends more than 10% of compensated time in one month on such services. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423(a)(1).

Eller is concerned about the steps it must take to ensure that certain employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political services. Eller will circulate a memorandum or other notice authorizing employees to work up to 10% of their time (no more than 16 hours) on political work. Eller currently has no method of determining what its employees in the field (i.e. installers, repairers) do. Is such a memorandum sufficient? Must Eller initiate time log sheets?

Incidentally, Eller will be reporting the payment of salary to some of its higher paid employees who will be spending more than 10% of their time on campaigns.

At the June 12, 1978, Opinion Request meeting, the staff concluded that a memorandum alone is not sufficient. Eller must maintain some kind of written record to ensure that its employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political work. (See letter to Nielsen.)

BC:plh

sections.question: Is a memorandum alone sufficient for Eller Outdoor Advertising to ensure employees do not exceed 10% of their compensated time on political activities? Must Eller initiate time log sheets?
sections.conclusion: A memorandum alone is not sufficient. Eller must maintain some kind of written record to ensure that its employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political work.
sections.question_synthetic: When a company authorizes employees to spend up to 10% of compensated time on political activities, is a memorandum sufficient to comply with reporting requirements, or must written time records be maintained?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that a memorandum authorization is insufficient and that Eller must maintain written records to track employee time spent on political activities to ensure compliance with the 10% threshold before contributions must be reported.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a memorandum summarizing FPPC staff conclusions from a June 12, 1978 Opinion Request meeting. The question and conclusion are clearly identifiable. Reference to an accompanying letter to Nielsen suggests related correspondence. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' headers present, but content structure is clear.

QUESTION: Is a memorandum alone sufficient for Eller Outdoor Advertising to ensure employees do not exceed 10% of their compensated time on political activities? Must Eller initiate time log sheets?

CONCLUSION: A memorandum alone is not sufficient. Eller must maintain some kind of written record to ensure that its employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political work.

Under §18423, Eller will have made a contribution or expenditure if an employee spends more than 10% of his compensated time working on political activities. The staff advised Eller that its employees should keep some type of written record to insure that those employees who do spend more than 10% of their compensated time working on political activities will be reported by Eller. **Memorandum** To: Opinion Request Participants Date: June 8, 1978 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Barbara Campbell Subject: Eller Outdoor Advertising's Employees' Services as Contributions As you may recall, several weeks ago we considered a request from Chip Nielsen concerning the reporting of employee salary payments as an in-kind contribution from Eller Outdoor Advertising ("Eller"). Eller will be identifying the specific elections for which it wants its employees to campaign in opposition to the measure. Adm. Code Section 18423(a)(1). Eller is concerned about the steps it must take to ensure that certain employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political services. Eller will circulate a memorandum or other notice authorizing employees to work up to 10% of their time (no more than 16 hours) on political work. Eller currently has no method of determining what its employees in the field (i.e. installers, repairers) do. Is such a memorandum sufficient? Must Eller initiate time log sheets? Incidentally, Eller will be reporting the payment of salary to some of its higher paid employees who will be spending more than 10% of their time on campaigns. At the June 12, 1978, Opinion Request meeting, the staff concluded that a memorandum alone is not sufficient. Eller must maintain some kind of written record to ensure that its employees do not exceed 10% of their time on political work. (See letter to Nielsen.) BC:plh
embedding.summary: The FPPC advised Eller Outdoor Advertising that while it may authorize employees to spend up to 10% of compensated time on political campaigns, a memorandum alone is insufficient; the company must maintain written records to document that employees do not exceed the 10% threshold, which triggers reporting requirements under California Administrative Code Section 18423(a)(1).
